title: Simulate Security Package Upgrades
hook: Security patch triage starts by seeing what apt would change, without changing it.
problem: You need to identify pending security updates on a Debian or Ubuntu host before a maintenance window.
starting_state: A Debian or Ubuntu host has apt metadata and possible security updates available.
what_changed: Nothing changes. apt-get runs in simulation mode and awk prints only install lines from security pockets.
when_to_use: Use before patch windows, incident response, or compliance notes when you need a read-only security update list.
when_not_to_use: Do not treat simulation output as a completed patch; it only describes what apt would try to do.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only simulation.
expected_output: Inst rows for packages whose candidate version comes from a security repository.

shorts.title: Preview security updates.
shorts.voiceover: Before installing anything, simulate the upgrade and filter for security-origin package changes.
shorts.caption: Security patch triage starts read-only.

linkedin.question: Do you capture apt simulation output before approving security patch windows?
linkedin.cta: Use this as the first read-only Debian and Ubuntu patch triage step.

youtube.description: Use apt-get simulation output to identify pending Debian or Ubuntu security updates.

experiments.variant_b: What security updates would apt install?

title: Prove a Package Candidate Is From Security
hook: The package name is not enough; the candidate repository tells the patch story.
problem: A package is upgradable and you need to confirm whether the candidate version comes from the security pocket.
starting_state: You know the package name and need installed, candidate, and repository source evidence.
what_changed: Nothing changes. apt-cache reads local apt metadata and sed extracts the version and security-origin lines.
when_to_use: Use when documenting why a specific package is being patched or when comparing update and security pockets.
when_not_to_use: Do not use it to install the package; this is only metadata inspection.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.
expected_output: Installed and candidate versions plus a security repository line for the candidate.

shorts.title: Verify the security candidate.
shorts.voiceover: For one package, apt-cache policy shows the installed version, candidate version, and where that candidate comes from.
shorts.caption: Patch evidence lives in apt policy.

linkedin.question: When reviewing a security patch, do you prove the candidate origin or just trust the package name?
linkedin.cta: Use apt-cache policy for package-level patch evidence.

youtube.description: Check whether a Debian or Ubuntu package candidate comes from a security repository.

experiments.variant_b: Installed vs candidate vs security pocket.

title: Find Held Packages Blocking Patches
hook: A held package can quietly keep a security update out of production.
problem: Patch simulation shows kept-back packages and you need to see whether apt holds are part of the reason.
starting_state: The host may have packages marked on hold through apt-mark.
what_changed: Nothing changes. apt-mark prints held package names and sed labels them for incident notes.
when_to_use: Use before blaming repositories, mirrors, or dependency conflicts for missing security updates.
when_not_to_use: Do not remove holds automatically; confirm why each package was held and who owns the risk.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.
expected_output: Held package names prefixed with held: for quick review.

shorts.title: Find patch blockers.
shorts.voiceover: If security patches are not moving, check held packages before assuming apt is broken.
shorts.caption: Held packages can block patching.

linkedin.question: Do your patch reviews include a held-package check?
linkedin.cta: Add apt-mark showhold to Debian and Ubuntu security triage.

youtube.description: Use apt-mark showhold to find Debian or Ubuntu packages that may block security updates.

experiments.variant_a: Held packages can hide patch debt.

title: Review Kept-Back Packages Before Patching
hook: Kept-back packages are where simple upgrade plans stop being simple.
problem: A normal apt upgrade simulation reports packages kept back, and you need to capture them before choosing a broader upgrade path.
starting_state: An apt upgrade simulation has packages that will not move under the current upgrade mode.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command prints the kept-back section from simulated apt output.
when_to_use: Use when kernel, agent, or dependency updates may need dist-upgrade, full-upgrade, or manual review.
when_not_to_use: Do not assume kept-back means safe to ignore; security fixes can be hidden behind dependency changes.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only simulation.
expected_output: The kept-back package section from apt simulation output.

shorts.title: Do not ignore kept-back packages.
shorts.voiceover: A normal upgrade can leave packages behind. Capture the kept-back list before deciding what kind of patch window you need.
shorts.caption: Kept-back means review, not ignore.

linkedin.question: How do you handle kept-back packages during security maintenance?
linkedin.cta: Record the kept-back section before changing upgrade strategy.

youtube.description: Extract kept-back packages from a simulated Debian or Ubuntu apt upgrade.

title: Dry-Run Unattended Security Upgrades
hook: Unattended upgrades can explain what they would patch before they patch it.
problem: You need to inspect what unattended-upgrades would select without installing packages.
command: unattended-upgrade --dry-run --debug 2>&1 | sed -n '/Packages that will be upgraded:/,/^$/p'
starting_state: A Debian or Ubuntu host uses unattended-upgrades or you are validating whether it would select security updates.
what_changed: Nothing changes. unattended-upgrade runs in dry-run mode and sed extracts the selected package summary.
when_to_use: Use when checking automatic patch coverage, security origins, or why a package was not auto-selected.
when_not_to_use: Do not run without --dry-run unless you intend to perform unattended package upgrades.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only dry-run output.
expected_output: A dry-run section listing packages that unattended-upgrades would upgrade.

commands: unattended-upgrade --dry-run --debug 2>&1 | sed -n '/Packages that will be upgraded:/,/^$/p'

shorts.title: Dry-run unattended upgrades.
shorts.voiceover: Before trusting automatic patching, dry-run unattended-upgrade and read what it would actually select.
shorts.caption: Automatic patching still needs evidence.

linkedin.question: Do you test unattended-upgrades with a dry run before relying on it?
linkedin.cta: Use this to validate automatic Debian and Ubuntu security patch coverage.

youtube.description: Use unattended-upgrade --dry-run --debug to inspect automatic security patch selection.

title: Find Services Needing Restart After Patches
hook: A patched library does not protect a process still using the old one.
problem: After security packages are applied, you need to see which services still need restarts.
starting_state: The host has needrestart available after package upgrades or patch simulation review.
what_changed: Nothing changes. needrestart prints batch-mode restart findings and sed extracts service restart commands.
when_to_use: Use after OpenSSL, libc, kernel, or web stack updates to plan service restarts and reboot decisions.
when_not_to_use: Do not restart services blindly from this output; coordinate impact and maintenance windows.
undo: No undo needed because this command only reports restart needs.
expected_output: systemctl restart commands for services that need to load patched libraries.

shorts.title: Patching is not always enough.
shorts.voiceover: After library updates, running services may still hold old code. Use needrestart to plan the restart step.
shorts.caption: Patch, then restart what still uses old libraries.

linkedin.question: Do your patch windows track service restarts separately from package installation?
linkedin.cta: Use needrestart output to close the gap between installed and active fixes.

youtube.description: Use needrestart batch output to find services needing restart after Debian or Ubuntu security updates.

experiments.variant_b: Find services still using old libraries.

title: Build a Recent Apt Patch Timeline
hook: Apt history turns patch claims into timestamps and package names.
problem: You need to prove what package changes happened recently and which command triggered them.
starting_state: A Debian or Ubuntu host has apt history logs from recent package operations.
what_changed: Nothing changes. awk filters apt history to the fields useful for patch timelines.
when_to_use: Use during incident response, audit follow-up, or post-maintenance validation.
when_not_to_use: Do not assume rotated logs are included; inspect compressed history files if you need older activity.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.
expected_output: Start dates, command lines, upgrade rows, and end dates from apt history.

shorts.title: Prove what apt changed.
shorts.voiceover: When someone says the server was patched, apt history gives you timestamps, commands, and upgraded packages.
shorts.caption: Apt history is your patch timeline.

linkedin.question: When validating patch work, do you check apt history or only current package versions?
linkedin.cta: Use apt history for timestamped package-change evidence.

youtube.description: Extract recent apt package-change timelines from /var/log/apt/history.log.

title: Find Warnings in Apt Terminal Logs
hook: The package installed, but the terminal log may still contain the warning that matters.
problem: A patch run completed and you need to spot warnings, errors, failed maintainer scripts, or restart clues.
starting_state: The apt terminal log contains recent package operation output.
what_changed: Nothing changes. grep filters the terminal log for warning and failure terms.
when_to_use: Use after unattended-upgrades, manual patching, or package repair to catch non-fatal but important output.
when_not_to_use: Do not treat a quiet grep as a complete health check; also verify services and package state.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.
expected_output: Matching warning, error, failed, or dpkg-related lines from apt terminal logs.

shorts.title: Read apt warnings.
shorts.voiceover: A package run can finish and still leave useful warnings. Search the apt terminal log before closing the patch ticket.
shorts.caption: Patch validation includes the terminal log.

linkedin.question: Do your patch checks include apt term.log warnings?
linkedin.cta: Search terminal logs before calling package maintenance clean.

youtube.description: Search /var/log/apt/term.log for warnings and failures after Debian or Ubuntu patching.

experiments.variant_b: Do not close patch work without logs.

title: Preview Security Impact of dist-upgrade
hook: Kernel and dependency security fixes may only appear in the broader upgrade plan.
problem: A regular upgrade leaves packages behind and you need to preview the broader resolver plan without installing it.
starting_state: Kept-back packages or dependency changes suggest that normal upgrade simulation is incomplete.
what_changed: Nothing changes. apt-get simulates dist-upgrade and awk lists proposed package installations or upgrades.
when_to_use: Use when kernel, libc, or dependency-chain security fixes are kept back by the normal upgrade path.
when_not_to_use: Do not run the real dist-upgrade casually; review removals, new packages, and service impact first.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only simulation.
expected_output: Inst rows showing the broader package resolver plan, including security-origin kernel or library updates.

shorts.title: Preview the broader patch plan.
shorts.voiceover: If normal upgrade keeps packages back, simulate dist-upgrade before deciding whether the maintenance window is big enough.
shorts.caption: Simulate broader resolver changes first.

linkedin.question: When do you escalate from apt upgrade to a broader dist-upgrade review?
linkedin.cta: Use simulation output to size the patch window before changing the host.

youtube.description: Use apt-get -s dist-upgrade to preview broader Debian or Ubuntu security patch impact.

experiments.variant_a: Kernel patches may need broader review.

title: Check Whether Patches Require Reboot
hook: Some security fixes are not complete until the host boots the new kernel or libraries.
problem: After package updates, you need to know whether the system is signaling a required reboot and which packages triggered it.
starting_state: A Debian or Ubuntu host may have reboot-required marker files after security updates.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command checks marker files and prints packages associated with the reboot requirement.
when_to_use: Use after kernel, libc, OpenSSL, or unattended security updates to plan reboot timing.
when_not_to_use: Do not reboot immediately from this output alone; coordinate service impact and maintenance policy.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.
expected_output: A reboot-required marker plus package names that requested a reboot.

shorts.title: Is reboot part of the patch?
shorts.voiceover: After security updates, check the reboot marker and package list so the patch window does not end too early.
shorts.caption: Patch completion may require reboot.

linkedin.question: Do your security patch runbooks separate package install, service restart, and reboot validation?
linkedin.cta: Use reboot-required markers to make the final step explicit.

youtube.description: Check Debian or Ubuntu reboot-required marker files after security patching.

experiments.variant_a: The patch window may need a reboot.
experiments.variant_b: Do not stop at package installed.
